sentences.text: Challenging, I would say more than satisfying.
I enjoyed-- I don't know, the words in German did not exist for me.
Yes, because at the age of 12, I suddenly found myself in a different atmosphere.
And the level of learning was much more demanding.
Yes, and I was youngest as far as I remember.
But challenging is a good word for me.
But in my class, there were about, I don't know, 50 or 60-- I couldn't tell you exactly.
It was boarding-- so-called boarding school, old-fashioned boarding school.
As early as possible. 6 o'clock in the morning.
They word recreation I don't remember.
I remember that I could not afford newspapers.
And I used to go to a corner where they sold newspapers, and used to pay a couple of grosh, whatever it is.
The newspapers were clipped so people should be able to open it and read it without paying.
This was an invention that was made in Poland a long time before here.
So that person who sold them, used to have one or two open.
And they used to give him something.
And I used to read, you know?
That was my father says, not very legal.
But I was interested, so I very political minded, besides from my sisters, I got it, you know-
Yes, yes, penetrated little by little.
Because I remember in the '30s was the Spanish Civil War.
And I, at that time already, sided with the left, with the Republicans.
So this gives me an idea at the age of 13--
I was political minded early then, yes.
But I was-- I'm a loner all my life, till today.
I live a lonely life, not-- not with my wife and children.
But from what I remember, their interests were not my interests.
To tell you honestly, I don't remember.
I left on my own volition, yes.
I decided this was not my way of life.
See, if-- if there would be excommunication in Jewish life, I was-- I would be doomed to excommunication.
But luckily, Jewish people cannot be excommunicated.
I didn't go back to-- to yeshiva.
My father wouldn't have tolerated it.
I remember vaguely, that I had a clash with my father.
That was the first time and the last time in my life.
But always-- I always accepted everything was dished out to me.
And I remember the saying when I left home, and I said, my foot will never step in here again.
And I never came in again.
That was addressed to my father.
So I never stepped in the house again.
That was the last time I with my parents-- with my father together.
The last word I spoke to him.
He ignored me, and I accepted it.
You mean the day-- the day when I left?
When I left home, I took a train and went to my father's brother.
In Warsaw, who was as religious as my father, or even more maybe.
And I told some fib that yeshiva is closed.
But I remember this night I slept there.
And early in the morning, I left because I was afraid my father would come to take-- fetch me.
I went, my mother had a sister in a city called Lodz.
It's the largest-- second largest city in Poland.
And I was there for quite a number of weeks.
I wouldn't have had any chance, because at that time, work-- to get work was not that simple.
After a while, I-- what I'm saying now is-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I remember vaguely.
I don't remember exactly-- I'm 73 now.
That was-- what is it? 14, and 59 years.
I remember I went back to Warsaw.
Yes, till the rage subsided from my father.
I was in communication with my mother.
I cannot answer because I don't remember.
I had never any conflict with my mother till the last day when I saw her, never any conflict whatsoever.
I remembered the first work was that I carried coal.
The basket with coals I used to carry.
Coal, this was for a while.
And then I went to work in a sweatshop.
Let me try to describe what I did.
That was some textile [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] jackets or sweaters.
Yes, I don't know the English word.
And I remember it was some kind of machines that used to make it like Angora.
And I fluffed it up [INAUDIBLE] the evening, till-- till the boss said go home.
And I-- I earned 5 zlotys a week as far as I remember.
And at that time an American dollar was 5 zloty.
So you can imagine I made an American dollar a week.
I don't remember the very beginning.
But I don't know if-- it come-- it came now a time when we had a great upheaval in our home.
And I would rather not-- not go into particulars, because after I left home, the whole family was involved in that so-called break up.
And I would like just to leave it at that.
Never developed-- never developed any more than just sympathy.
I was never active, I mean, in any-- you have to realize that the Communist Party was illegal in Poland, when just for being a member of any activity we went to jail.
And I was not for that-- made up for that kind of life.
My older sister was a member.
From '37 to '39 of when the Second World War break out-- broke out, sorry.
Oh, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I knew-- I remember in 1933, when I was 10 years old, I remember that Hitler came to power.
And I remember that I was so keen on leaving Poland, because-- I couldn't anticipate, of course, the Holocaust.
But I knew the disaster was coming-- disaster.
Hitler never made a secret of it that he was so vehemently anti-Jewish.
He didn't, of course, say he was going to burn us alive.
He sprang a surprise in this respect.
But I knew dangerous, at age of 10, I knew that enormous danger was coming.
But we were helpless, couldn't do anything.
Anschluss, Crystal Night, Anschluss, Crystal Night, in chronological order.
I am not so sure about that.
Mine, because I am-- I was Polish, into [INAUDIBLE] politics, and I could see the dark clouds.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the bombing started.
The war broke out without announcing, you know.
I remember people saying these are just exercises.
Yes bombing, bomb the city on Warsaw.
And I remember coming to the railway station.
It was hard to get a train back to Falenica.
Where my mother was, my sisters were.
But that lasted two or three weeks, as we were on the left side of the Vistula that divided Warsaw in--
The Germans marched in-- into that Falenica place about seven or eight days before Warsaw was taken.
And that-- they set afire Falenica.
And we were sitting in the cellar.
My mother, my sisters, and I.
My brother stayed with his father.
And I remember I was thinking the choice of either being burned alive or just jump out and face the Germans.
The women were left to go where they wanted.
As a matter of fact, my mother, my sisters went to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother went to her sister that lived in that place called Otwock, not far away, a very short distance.
And men, including myself-- I was 16 years old-- were taken [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] for a march-- a long march-- I would say about 30, 40 kilometers-- to a place called Minsk.
I don't think the SS was at that time.
I didn't remember the word even exists.
It took a long time till we arrived in [SIGHS] in Minsk.
And we were all marched into a place where they used to exercise horses.
A few days, a few days.
While we were there, we were hungry, and we were beaten by the Poles.
--a Polish group and a Jewish group?
But I remember when they let me out, I was blood at my head.
I went to the place where my mother was staying.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were on our way to Russia.
The beginning-- the part of Poland that was occupied by Russia, yes.
Wait for-- wait to be registered, and sended-- send away with the Russians to another place.
It was a few weeks, I was there.
My mom was still in Poland under German occupation.
So she came a few weeks later.
She joined me in a city called Babruysk.
This is-- Belarus it's called now.
I was taken, registered and sent away to a city.
That was a large factory that made doors and windows.
I was just put in a place and-- and worked.
Later, yes, a few weeks later.
My sister went to another place.
They both went to different places, yes.
Stayed all through the bitter end.
The basics, not more than the basics.
At the begin-- at the beginning, before my mother came, I was together with other single people, eight or 10 in a place where we just had a place to sleep.
Yes, we didn't suffer from any shortage of food.
Work was hard work for a boy of 16.
At the begin-- at the beginning very grateful, because I escaped the Nazis.
I-- I started seeing the false, but you cannot express yourself in Russia.
They used to say in Russia words-- I don't know where we heard a joke-- that in Soviet Russia, the only place you can open your mouth is at the dentist.
No jokes, no sayings, no criticisms, nothing.
By that time we got a little room.
It was a long corridor, you know, with single rooms.
All the time working in the timber, yes.
I don't remember having-- till I escaped, till the Nazis marched in and the beginning of the real war in '41-- having been out of this place.
The war broke out in 1941, the 22nd of June.
Five days later we heard that the Nazis are coming in.
And whoever can save, my mother told me, to leave.
And we didn't believe that the Russians would be defeated so quickly.
They always presented themselves as a mighty power.
And suddenly the-- the Germans are coming close and closer.
What can I to tell you?
I remember only coming-- arriving with sores on my feet from running so much, and coming to the train, and pushing myself in the cattle trains, trying to escape.
--taking out people who have wanted to escape further inside Russia.
On the whole there were more Jewish than non-Jewish.
People who are political affiliated, non-Jews were running as well, because they were afraid more.
And Jews, because they were-- because they were Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't have exact reports.
But what I heard from Babruysk, from what I heard later, they were put into wooden houses and burned alive.
You have to realize one things.
The Jews-- even the Jews who were in different categories, the ones who were living not under communist regime were tortured to death.
The ones who were living in Russia were immediately killed.
Never heard of them any more, anything.
They didn't have anywhere to go.
As far as I know, he-- he-- there was an action in Falenica in 19-- in September '42.
And they were sent to Treblinka.
Last day I had any-- seen any family, close family, in my life, at the ripe age of 18.
I know that they took us-- the train went further, deep into Russia to city called Tambov.
It was start and stop, and collecting more people, you see.
All the time, but-- there was so much suffering.
There was no air, and no water, and no food, so-- was taken.
At that time, actually, we were so afraid, that we didn't know what to-- what to fear first, and what to suffer first.
[SIGHS] From Tambov, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was sent to a city called Magnitogorsk.
Ural Mountains, on the way to Siberia, before Siberia, yes.
That was a large city that worked only for military purposes.
In Russia, you don't know-- you don't know these things.
[LAUGHING] I didn't-- didn't go to ask them, what do you do?
A large city-- actually, this city was built by the Communists.
A new city in the Urals was built.
I would say by then it was mid-summer.
